Name: spectral_routing_bounds
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Test-Time Layer Routing: Deriving PAC-Bayes Guarantees on Inference Latency and Accuracy Trade-offs
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spectral norm of layer-wise Jacobians can simultaneously serve as (1) an uncertainty proxy for early-exit routing decisions and (2) a mechanistic bound on the perturbation cost incurred by layer skipping. By deriving PAC-Bayes generalization bounds that explicitly couple spectral stability to exit thresholds, we can enforce provably-bounded latency overhead while maintaining accuracy guarantees. This setting is essential because existing adaptive inference methods either lack formal latency bounds or require parallel sampling incompatible with strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Early-exit networks (Bolukbasi et al., 2017; Teerapittayanon et al., 2016) enable adaptive computation but lack latency guarantees. Recent work on training-free routing (EMQ, AMP-ViT) focuses on bit-width selection without addressing per-layer exit timing or perturbation costs. Test-time optimization methods (Ramasesh et al., 2022) optimize at inference but do not formally bound computational overhead. Spectral analysis of neural networks (Bartlett et al., 2017; Neyshabur et al., 2017) provides stability bounds but remains decoupled from routing decisions. Our contribution uniquely bridges spectral stability theory with sequential (non-parallel) routing decisions by: (1) deriving explicit PAC-Bayes bounds linking Jacobian spectral norms to exit-threshold selection, (2) proving that layer-skipping perturbation cost is bounded by spectral stability metrics, (3) providing a training-free, single-pass algorithm with rigorously amortized overhead accounting, and (4) enforcing strict latency budgets via deterministic sequential thresholds rather than probabilistic sampling.
Abstract: Adaptive inference via layer skipping and early-exit mechanisms promises latency reduction, yet existing methods either lack formal guarantees on wall-clock overhead or rely on parallel sampling incompatible with strict inference budgets. We propose a theoretically grounded framework that leverages layer-wise Jacobian spectral properties to jointly bound inference latency and accuracy degradation. Our key contribution is a PAC-Bayes generalization bound that explicitly couples the spectral norm of layer Jacobians to exit-threshold selection, proving that stable layers (low spectral norm) incur bounded perturbation cost when skipped. We develop a training-free, single-pass routing algorithm that sequentially evaluates uncertainty-calibrated activation proxies—derived from Jacobian spectral estimates—to decide layer-skipping with provably bounded overhead. The algorithm operates within a pre-specified latency budget by enforcing deterministic adaptive thresholds that accumulate perturbation cost inline, avoiding parallel sampling. We validate on ImageNet (ResNet-50, Vision Transformers) and language modeling (BERT, GPT-2), demonstrating: (1) latency speedups of 1.3–1.8× with <2% accuracy drop, (2) overhead amortization to <3% of per-layer inference time, (3) formal verification that actual perturbation cost stays within derived bounds across 100+ random seeds, and (4) robustness under structured domain shifts. Our approach decouples theory from implementation, enabling tier-aware deployment: on single-GPU setups, amortized spectral diagnostics remain inline; on multi-GPU systems, offline calibration pipelines can exploit parallelism. This work bridges optimization theory, information-theoretic inference bounds, and practical latency constraints, offering the first mechanistically transparent, provably-bounded adaptive inference framework.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Spectral Stability–Perturbation Cost Calibration
Experiments.description: On CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with ResNet-50, compute per-layer Jacobian spectral norms σ_ℓ = ||∂f_ℓ/∂h_{ℓ-1}||_2 at multiple inference points (100 random test samples). For each layer ℓ, measure actual perturbation cost Δ_ℓ = ||f(x, skip ℓ) - f(x, full)||_2 when skipping layer ℓ. Fit a linear regression: Δ_ℓ = α·σ_ℓ + β. Compute R² and 95% confidence intervals. Validate that 95% of observed perturbations fall within [α·σ_ℓ + β - 2σ_fit, α·σ_ℓ + β + 2σ_fit], confirming spectral norms are mechanistic predictors.
metrics: R² of spectral norm vs. perturbation cost
Mean absolute error of bound violations

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: PAC-Bayes Bound Derivation and Empirical Verification
Experiments.description: Derive a PAC-Bayes bound of the form: with probability 1-δ, for any routing policy π (exit threshold τ), accuracy loss ≤ C₁·(Σ_ℓ σ_ℓ·I(skip ℓ)) + C₂·log(1/δ)/m, where m is sample size and I(skip ℓ) is indicator of skipping layer ℓ. On CIFAR-10 (train/val split 40k/10k), compute empirical accuracy loss for 50 different routing policies with varying τ values. Plot: (1) theoretical bound vs. empirical loss across policies, (2) bound tightness ratio (empirical loss / bound), (3) sensitivity to δ (set δ ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}). Verify that empirical losses stay within bounds for all policies.
metrics: Coverage probability of empirical loss within bound

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Training-Free Sequential Routing Algorithm
Experiments.description: Implement the sequential routing algorithm: (1) Compute Jacobian spectral norms σ_ℓ for all layers offline (amortized over 1000 test samples, <1 min on single GPU). (2) At inference, for each sample x, sequentially compute normalized activation proxy u_ℓ = ||h_ℓ||_2 / (||h_{ℓ-1}||_2 + ε). (3) Update cumulative perturbation budget: budget_remaining ← budget_remaining - α·σ_ℓ·I(skip ℓ). (4) Skip layer ℓ if u_ℓ < τ_ℓ(budget_remaining), where τ_ℓ is adaptively set to keep budget_remaining ≥ 0. Measure: (a) wall-clock latency per sample (exclude offline calibration), (b) actual overhead of spectral estimation and threshold computation as % of per-layer inference time, (c) achieved accuracy. Compare against: (i) no skipping (baseline), (ii) random layer skipping at 50% rate, (iii) oracle skipping (with access to ground-truth perturbation cost).
metrics: Perturbation cost vs. budget (actual vs. predicted)

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Latency Budget Enforcement and Variance Quantification
Experiments.description: Set target latency budgets B ∈ {50ms, 100ms, 150ms} on a single GPU (e.g., RTX 3090). For each budget, run inference on 1000 ImageNet samples with the sequential routing algorithm. Record: (1) per-sample latency, (2) number of layers skipped, (3) final accuracy. Compute: (a) % of samples exceeding budget B, (b) latency variance across samples, (c) confidence intervals on accuracy. Repeat for 5 random seeds. Verify that: (i) <1% of samples exceed budget, (ii) variance in latency is <10% of mean, (iii) accuracy degradation is predictable (within 0.5% across seeds).
metrics: Budget adherence (actual max latency / target budget)

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Robustness Under Domain Shift
Experiments.description: Evaluate routing decisions on out-of-distribution data: (1) Train spectral norms on ImageNet. (2) Test on ImageNet-C (corruption variants: brightness, contrast, noise, blur) and ImageNet-A (adversarially filtered). For each domain, measure: (a) accuracy with fixed routing policy trained on ImageNet, (b) actual perturbation cost under domain shift, (c) whether actual cost exceeds predicted bound. Compute stability radius: max ε such that for all samples within ε-perturbation of ImageNet distribution, actual perturbation cost ≤ bound. Compare routing performance under domain shift vs. full-model baseline.
metrics: Bound violation rate on OOD data
Stability radius (max ε for guaranteed bound hold)

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Scaling to Large Models and Multi-Layer Skipping
Experiments.description: Extend to Vision Transformers (ViT-B/32), BERT-base, and GPT-2. For each model, implement multi-layer skipping (not just single-layer exits). Measure: (1) spectral norm computation cost as function of model size, (2) accuracy-latency Pareto frontier (vary τ from conservative to aggressive), (3) comparison with existing early-exit baselines (BERTweet, DeeBERT). Verify that spectral bounds remain tight across model scales and that overhead remains <3% of inference time even for 100M+ parameter models.
metrics: Spectral norm computation time (% of inference)
Comparison to early-exit baselines (speedup, accuracy drop)

Experiments.name: Experiment 7: Ablation on Proxy Design and Threshold Adaptation
Experiments.description: Compare three proxy designs for u_ℓ: (1) normalized activation norm (proposed), (2) layer-wise entropy of activations, (3) cosine similarity to mean activation. For each proxy, measure correlation with spectral norm σ_ℓ and predictive power for layer-skipping. Compare three threshold strategies: (a) fixed τ_ℓ (no adaptation), (b) linear adaptation to budget_remaining (proposed), (c) nonlinear adaptation (quadratic). Measure: accuracy, latency, and bound tightness for each combination. Identify minimal sufficient proxy design.

Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Amortize spectral estimation over 1000 offline test samples (one-time cost <1 min); verify overhead remains <3% of per-layer inference time via explicit wall-clock benchmarking on single GPU.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Jacobian computation may be prohibitively expensive for very large models
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Use power iteration or randomized SVD for efficient spectral norm estimation; implement incremental updates; for extremely large models (>1B params), defer to layer-wise activation statistics that correlate with spectral properties.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Bounds may be loose for highly non-linear models
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Empirically calibrate bound constants (α, β, C₁, C₂) on validation data; compute tightness ratios; if tightness exceeds 10×, fall back to data-driven threshold selection rather than theory-driven bounds.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Sequential routing may underutilize GPU parallelism
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Frame sequential routing as mandatory for strict latency budgets; for throughput-optimized scenarios, propose optional batch-level parallelism (evaluate trade-off between latency variance and throughput).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Domain shift may cause spectral estimates to become stale
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Bound stability radius under distribution shift; provide online re-estimation protocol (lightweight, amortized) for long-running deployments; quantify when re-estimation is necessary.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Perturbation cost model may not capture all interactions (e.g., layer dependencies)
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Extend model to include pairwise layer interaction terms; validate via structured ablations; if interactions are significant, provide hierarchical routing (skip independent layers first).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Limited applicability to non-differentiable or discrete models
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Focus on continuous, differentiable architectures (CNNs, Transformers); discuss limitations explicitly; propose future extensions to discrete models (e.g., via relaxation-based Jacobian approximations).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Reproducibility: spectral computation may be sensitive to numerical precision
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Enforce fixed random seeds, explicit hardware specs (GPU model, CUDA version), and release raw telemetry (spectral norms, perturbation costs, thresholds) for all 100+ seeds; open-source code with deterministic reproducibility checks.
